Facts.text: Oldest Chinese recording of a solar eclipse
Henry III becomes Holy Roman Emperor
Roquefort cheese created in a cave near Roquefort, France
Rome-Innocentius II crowns Lotharius III Roman-German emperor
The "Peace of Ath", signed by Count Louis II of Flanders and Duke Wenceslaus of Luxembourg ends the attempt of the succession of Brabant
Mob led by Ferrand Martinez surrounds and sets fire to the Jewish quarter of Seville in Spain, the surviving Jews are sold into slavery
Lord Lovell and John de la Pole's army lands at Furness, Lancashire
The steeple of St Paul's Cathedral, the tallest structure in London, is struck by lightning, catching fire and destroying its bells and roof
Dutch East India ship Batavia wrecks on Morning Reef off the Houtman Abrolhos, Western Australia, with 200 survivors (only 70 survive after three months due to mutiny and murders)
Dutch Stadtholder William Frederick conquers Dijlerschans
Battle at Dunkirk: English vs Dutch fleet
Pageant of 1671: French claim possession of lands "from the northern and western seas to the southern sea, including lands yet to be discovered" in ceremony at Jesuit Mission, Sault Saint, Canada
Prussia goes to the Covenant of Nymphenburg
Quakers leave assembly of Pennsylvania
The Great Upheaval: New England planters arrive in Nova Scotia, Canada, to claim land taken from the Acadians
A transit of Venus is followed five hours later by a total solar eclipse, the shortest such interval in history
Joseph and Jacques Montgolfier make the first public hot-air balloon flight (unmanned), covering 2 km and lasting 10 minutes with an estimated altitude of 1,600-2,000 m
Madame Elizabeth Thible becomes the first female balloonist
Captain George Vancouver claims Puget Sound for Britain
Grieving over the death of his wife, Marie Clotilde of France, King Charles Emmanuel IV of Sardinia abdicates his throne in favor of his brother, Victor Emmanuel
Louisiana Territory officially renamed "Missouri Territory"
First free press (without government approval) founded in Australia - the Hobart Town Gazette by ex-convict Andrew Bent
Unseasonable hurricane hits NYC
National Congress selects Leopold von Saksen-Coburg as King of Belgium
3rd national black convention meets (Philadelphia)
First baseball-type game in Canada played at Beachville, Upper Canada
Empire Engine Company No. 1 organized, in San Francisco, California
Self-deodorizing fertilizer patented in England
Confederates evacuate Fort Pillow, Tennessee
Van Bosse/Fock government begins
1st contract workers of British-Indies Co arrive in Suriname
Pacific Stock Exchange opens
Transcontinental Express arrives in San Francisco, California, via the First Transcontinental Railroad only 83 hours and 39 minutes after having left New York City
Cyprus ceded by Turkey to Britain for administrative purposes
Boston Beaneaters MLB pitcher John Clarkson is first to throw an "immaculate inning" (strikes out 3 batters on 9 pitches) in a 4-2 win over Philadelphia Quakers at South End Grounds
Oil City and Titusville, Pennsylvania, destroyed by oil tank explosion; 130 die
Cone of Mount Katmai (Alaska) collapses
State of Massachusetts passes 1st US minimum wage law
General Aleksei Brusilov begins a massive Russian offensive on the Eastern Front (WWI)
American men begin registering for the draft
Laura E. Richards and Maud Howe Elliott awarded 1st Pulitzer Prize for Biography (Julia Ward Howe), the story of the authors. mother
Most Excellent Order of British Empire inaugurated by KingGeorge Vto recognise the efforts of his people in WWI
French troops, with the aid of US troops, stop the Germans at Chateau-Thierry as they attempt to cross the Marne
US Congress passes the Women's Suffrage Bill, the 19th Amendment
US marines invade Costa Rica
Peace of Trianon between Allies & Hungary
Ignacy Mościcki becomes President of Poland (holds office till 1939)
President of the Republic of China Zhang Zuolin is assassinated by Japanese agents
Chilean coup led by Colonel Marmaduke Grove against President Juan Esteban Montero
1st night baseball game at Forbes Field in Pittsbugh, Pennsylvania, Pirates win 14-2 over the Boston Braves
1st NL night game at Sportsman's Park (Dodgers 10, Cardinals 1)
Republic of Croatia orders all Jews to wear a star with the letter Z
Battle of Midway begins; Japan's 1st major defeat in WW II
Capitol Record Co opens for business
St Louis Card Mort Cooper pitches his 2nd consecutive 1 hitter
French GeneralCharles de Gaullearrives in London
U-505 becomes the first German submarine captured and boarded on high seas by the US Navy, off the coast of North Africa; capture is kept secret until after the war
6th US Marine division occupies Orokoe Peninsula Okinawa
Largest solar prominence (300,000 miles/500,000 kms) observed
US House of Representatives approves Taft-Hartley act
CVP wins Belgian parliamentary election
Dutch cyclist Wim van Est wins Bordeaux-Paris (586 km in 17:25)
Pirate's' Gus Bell hits for cycle helps beat Phillies 12-4
Arthur Murray flies X-1A rocket plane to record 27,000 m
France grants Vietnam independence inside French Union
1st commercial coal pipeline placed in operation
San Francisco Giants Hank Sauer and Bob Schmidt are first in NL history to hit consecutive pinch-hit HRs in 10-9 win over the Milwaukee Braves
US President John F. Kennedy and Soviet leaderNikita Khrushchevmeet at the Vienna Summit in Austria
First broadcast of "Pop Go the Beatles" on BBC radio
Maldives adopts constitution
Test Cricket debut of Geoff Boycott v Australia at Trent Bridge, 48
-10] Hurricane Alma, kills 51 in Honduras
Novelty single "Batman & His Grandmother" by Dickie Goodman hits #70
KTVN TV channel 2 in Reno, NV (CBS) begins broadcasting
Stockport Air Disaster: British Midland flight G-ALHG crashes in Hopes Carr, Stockport, killing 72 passengers and crew
22-year-old man sneaks into wheel pod of a jet parked in Havana and survives 9-hr flight to Spain despite thin oxygen levels at 29,000 ft
43rd National Spelling Bee: Libby Childress wins spelling croissant
MLB San Diego Padres draft Mike Ivie #1
Tonga (formerly Friendly Islands) declares independence from the United Kingdom
WSMW TV channel 27 in Worcester, MA (IND) begins broadcasting
Dutch politician Joseph Luns is appointed Secretary-General of NATO
Oakland A's beat Washington Senators, 5-3, in 21 innings
Angela Davis, African American activist, acquitted of killing a white guard
Patent for the ATM is granted to Don Wetzel, Tom Barnes and George Chastain
Never repeated 10 cent Beer Night at Cleveland, unruly fans stumble onto field and cause Indians to forfeit the game to Rangers with score tied 5-5 in 9th
NFL grants franchise to Seattle Seahawks
Saudi Arabia announces that it will increase its participation in Aramco to 60 percent
Oldest animal fossils in US discovered in North Carolina
"The Architecture of Luis Barragán" exhibition opens at MOMA, New York bring the Mexican architect's work to international notice
An estimated 20,000 Scottish football fans invade the Wembley Stadium pitch after Scotland beats England, 2-1; goalposts and advertising hoardings destroyed
Violence during Puerto Rican Day in Chicago kills 2
32nd Tony Awards: "Da" (play) and "Ain't Misbehavin'" (musical) win
Liberal Julio Turbay Ayola wins Colombia elections
South African President John Vorster resigns due to a scandal
54th National Spelling Bee: Paige Pipkin wins spelling sarcophagus
"Star Trek II: The Wrath of Khan," released in USA
Israel attacks targets in south Lebanon
DNA is successfully cloned from an extinct animal
MLB Draft: New York Mets take Mechanicsburg Area Senior HS outfielder Shawn Abner at #1
STS 51-G vehicle moves to launch pad
Supreme Court strikes down Alabama "moment of silence" law
Jonathan Pollard, spy for Israel, pleads guilty in US court
American hurdler Edwin Moses' 122 race winning streak which extends to nearly 10 years ends when he is beaten by countryman Danny Harris in Madrid, Spain
42nd Tony Awards: "M. Butterfly" (play) and "The Phantom of the Opera" (musical) win
Longest game in Baltimore Memorial Stadium (5:46) 14 inn (beat NY 7-6)
2nd Children's Miracle Network Telethon raises $770,000
Beijing policeman shoots & wounds Chinese priemer Li Ping
Eastern Europe's first partially free elections in 40 years are held in Poland, Solidarity Party comes to power
Gas explodes near 2 passenger trains in USSR, kills 100s
Largest parade in Bronx history honors 350th anniversary
Red Sox lead Blue Jays 10-0 in 7th, but lose 12-11 in 12 for Blue Jays 12th consecutive victory at Fenway
Greyhound Bus files bankruptcy
LA Dodger Ramon Martinez strikes out 18 Atlanta Braves
NY Telephone company announces that it wants Bronx area code 917
1st post WW II non-communist government in Albania
Robert S Strauss becomes US ambassador to Soviet Union
San Jose voters reject Giants plan to build a new stadium
United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change (UNFCCC) is signed in New York
Haile Gebre Selassie runs world record 5 km (12:56.96)
49th Tony Awards: "Love! Valour! Compassion!" (play) & "Sunset Boulevard" (musical) win
8th Children's Miracle Network Telethon raises $1,331,000
New Zealand creates world record score for a Rugby Union international in thrashing Japan, 145-17 in the World Cup in Bloomfontein, RSA; Marc Ellis 6 tries, Simon Culhane 20 conversions

UN Security renews its "oilforfood" initiative whereby Iraq may sell $2 billion worth of oil to buy food, medicine and other necessities to alleviate civilian suffering under the sanctions imposed when it invaded Kuwait in 1990
Terry Nichols is sentenced to life in prison for his role in the Oklahoma City bombing
54th Tony Awards: "Copenhagen" (play) and "Contact" (musical) win
Gyanendra, the last King of Nepal, ascends to the throne after the massacre in the Royal Palace
Panamanian jockey Eddie Castro sets a North American record with 9 wins on the 13-race card at Calder Race Course in Miami, Florida
Stanley Cup Final, Civic Center, Pittsburgh, PA: Detroit Red Wings defeat Pittsburgh Penguins, 3-2 for a 4-2 series win; Red Wings' 11th Championship
Car bomb kills 26 and injures 190 people in central Baghdad, Iraq
Japan's stock market plummets to record lows with the S&P/TOPIX 150 reaching its lowest level since 1983
MLB Draft: Puerto Rico Academy & HS shortstop Carlos Correa first pick by Houston Astros
US drone attack kills 15 militants in Pakistan, including high ranking al-Qaeda official, Abu Yahya al-Libi
Wedding party bus crashes killing 23 and injuring 60 people in Islamabad, Pakistan
10 Nigerian generals and five other senior military officers are court-martialed for providing arms and information to jihadist terrorist group Boko Haram
Australian cricket batsman Adam Voges becomes the oldest player to hit a century on debut in 1st Test v West Indies in Roseau; 35-year-old Voges scores 130 not out during Australia's 9 wicket win
Jordanian Prime Minister Hani Mulki resigns amid huge protests against tax and price increases. King Abdullah appoints Omar Razzaz to replace him.
Deforestation of the Amazon forest in Brazil the fastest for a decade as 740 square kilometers cleared in 30 days according to Brazilian space research institute
Former US school security guard Scot Peterson arrested and charged with neglect of a child and culpable negligence for not confronting gunman during Parkland school massacre in a landmark case
Movement in Japan to end compulsory wearing of high heels in work places trends with hashtag #KuToo
Over 100,000 people mark the 30th anniversary of Beijing's Tiananmen Square Massacre in Hong Kong and around the world
Professional gambler James Holzhauer's 32-game winning streak ends on "Jeopardy", just short of Ken Jenning's record $2.52M earnings
SF MLB manager Bruce Bochy reaches 1,000 win mark with the club as Giants beat Mets, 9-3 in NYC; second after legendary John McGraw to reach milestone
State of Emergency declared after 20,000 tonnes of diesel oil spills near Russian city of Norilsk, Siberia within the Arctic Circle
Thousands ignore a recent ban and mark the anniversary of Tiananmen Square Massacre in Hong Kong
UN report criticizing human rights violations in the Philippines, including deaths of 8,000 during its war on drugs, a day after county's House of Representatives passed new anti-terrorism law
Nepalese Prime Minister KP Sharma Oli makes a desperate plea for vaccines amid his country's devastating COVID-19 second wave, reporting nearly 9,000 daily cases late May
WHO says Monkeypox now found in 27 countries where not already endemic - 780 in last three weeks. Accesses global risk as moderate.
20,500 people are evacuated in Cologne, Germany, while experts defuse three huge unexploded WWII bombs recently discovered during preparations for road construction
Google signs agreement with Chile to build the first submarine fiber-optic cable connecting South America and Australia
Vietnam formally ends two-child policy, in effect since 2009, amid record-low birth rates
Siege of Osaka: Forces under the shogunTokugawa Ieyasutake Osaka Castle in Japan
Frederick Henry, Prince of Orange conquers Venlo
English Parliamentary army under Cornet George Joyce takes KingCharles Ias a prisoner during Second Civil War
Battle at Hohenfriedberg Silezie:Frederick the Great(Prussia) defeats Austrians and Saxons
Second Italian War of Independence: Battle of Magenta, results in a French-Sardinian victory underNapoleon IIIover the Austrians under Marshal Ferencz Gyulai
Pasteur Institute founded by French biologistLouis PasteurandÉmile Rouxin Paris
Henry Forddrives his first Ford through the streets of Detroit

English suffragetteEmily Davisonis trampled and mortally wounded by a racechorse called Anmer, ridden by Herbert Jones and owned by KingGeorge V, during running of the Derby at Epsom Downs in Surrey; Jones thrown from horse, horse finishes the race jockey-less, Davison dies from her injuries 4 days later.
1st Ryder Cup Golf, Worcester CC: US beats Great Britain, 9½-2½;Walter Hagenfirst American captain; Ted Ray first GB skipper
Edouard Herriot becomes Premier of France
Dr.Frederick Bantingis knighted for the co-discovery of insulin which revolutionized the treatment of diabetes and saved countless lives
Léon Blumbecomes France's first Socialist and first Jewish Prime Minister as head of People's Front government
British Prime MinisterWinston Churchilldelivers his famous "We shall fight on the seas and oceans" speech to the UK House of Commons
"Mrs Miniver" based on the novel by Jan Struther, directed byWilliam Wylerand starringGreer Garsonand Walter Pidgeon is released in the US (Best Picture 1943)
Argentina taken over by General Rawson & ColonelJuan Perón
US 5th Army enters and liberates Rome - first European Fascist city to be liberated
"Cavalcade of Stars" television variety program debuts (DuMont);Jackie Gleasonmade host in 1950
Pittsburgh pirates trade outfielderRalph Kiner& catcherJoe Garagiolato Chicago Cubs
"Mickey RooneyShow" TV comedy last airs on NBC
Soviet leaderNikita Khrushchev's secret speech "On the Cult of Personality and Its Consequences" criticizing Joseph Stalin is made public
French Prime MinisterCharles de Gaullearrives in Algiers
Lee Harvey Oswalddeparts Rotterdam on SS Maasdam to US
LA Dodgers pitcherSandy Koufaxtosses his 3rd career no-hitter; beats Phillies, 3-0 in Philadelphia

The Beatles first (and only) "World Tour" begins with two ten-song shows at 4,400 seat KB Halle in Copenhagen, Denmark; British drummer Jimmie Nicol replacesRingo Starr(recovering from tonsillitis) for the first five dates (10 concerts + a Dutch TV appearance)
19th Emmy Awards: "Mission Impossible", "The Monkees", Don Knotts &Lucille Ballwin
Curt Flood's record 568 straight chances without an error ends (227 straight games)
Don Drysdalepitches his 6th straight shutout, en route to 58 innings
Beatles release "The Ballad OfJohn & Yoko" (recorded by just John and Paul), backed withGeorge Harrison's song, "Old Brown Shoe" in the US
Joe Clarkis sworn in as the youngest Prime Minister in Canadian history
Columbia Records releasesBruce Springsteen's seventh studio album "Born in the USA," his biggest commercial success that topped the charts in 11 countries and sold over 30 million copies worldwide
MLB New York Yankees outfielderRickey Hendersonsteals 2 bases for team record 249
43rd Tony Awards: "The Heidi Chronicles" (play) and "Jerome Robbins' Broadway" (musical) win
DrJack Kevorkianassists an Oregon woman to commit suicide, beginning a national debate over the right to die
PopeJohn Paul IIcompares abortion with Nazi murders
USPS announces 1.2 million people voted to choose the artwork on theirElvis Presleystamp, with 75% preferring a portrait of youngElvisover old Elvis
Canadian singerAvril Lavignereleases debut album, "Let Go"
DesignerRalph Laurenis presented with the American Fashion Legend Award byOprah Winfreyat the CFDA Awards in New York
British TV presenterMichael Parkinsonis knighted by Queen Elizabeth at Buckingham Palace
Ariana Grandeheadlines benefit concert for victims of her Manchester concert bombing, also featuringColdplay,Miley Cyrus,Justin Bieber, andKaty Perry
"Ocean's 8" film premieres, directed by Gary Ross starringSandra Bullock,Cate Blanchett,Anne HathawayandRihanna
Former US PresidentBill ClintonandJames Pattersonpublish a thriller novel "The President is Missing" together
Former US PresidentBill Clintonin interview with NBC says he hasn't and doesn't need to apologize toMonica Lewinsky
Biggest protests in Prague since the fall of communism by tens of thousands against Prime Minister Andrej Babis' use of EU subsidies
Memorial forGeorge Floydled by Rev.Al Sharpton, killed in police custody, in Minneapolis, as 10th night of protests at his death held around the country
Ukrainian counteroffensive begins with increased fighting in the east and south of the country
Indian Prime MinisterNarendra Modi's Bharatiya Janata party wins the most seats but not a majority, in the country's general election
US PresidentJoe Bidenintroduces immediate restrictions on the southern border, limiting illegal migrant crossings to 2,500 a day

British complete the "Miracle of Dunkirk" by evacuating 338,226 Allied troops from France via a flotilla of over 800 vessels, including Royal Navy destroyers, merchant marine boats, fishing boats, pleasure craft, and even lifeboats
GeneralEisenhowercancels planned D-Day invasion on June 5th after receiving unfavorable weather reports

American guitaristJimi Hendrixcovers new Beatles song "Sgt. Pepper's Lonely Hearts Club Band" at Saville Theatre (London), just days after its release, with George Harrison and Paul McCartney in the audience
Tiananmen Square Massacre: Chinese troops clear the square of student protesters, unofficial figures place death toll near 1,000
Philippa of England, Queen of Denmark, Norway and Sweden, born in Peterborough Castle, Cambridgeshire (d. 1430)
Antoine, Duke of Lorraine, born in Bar-le-Duc, France (d. 1544)
Claudia de' Medici, Grand Duchess of Tuscany, born in Palazzo Pitti, Florence, Tuscany (d. 1648)
Zacharie Robutel de La Noue, Canadian soldier, born in Montreal, Quebec (d. 1733)
François Quesnay, French personal physician of Louis XIV, born in Méré, Versailles, France (d. 1774)
Jacob Israel Emden [Jacob ben Tswi], German rabbi, born in Altona, Duchy of Holstein, Holy Roman Empire (now Germany) (d. 1776)
Theodoor Verhaegen, Flemish sculptor, born in Mechelen, Belgium (d. 1759)
Nikolaj Eigtved, Danish architect (Christiansborg Castle), born in Egtved, Denmark (d. 1754)
Benjamin Huntsman, English inventor and manufacturer, born in Lincolnshire, England (d. 1776)
Patrick Ferguson, Scottish army officer and rifle inventor (Ferguson flintlock rifle), born in Pitfours, Scotland (d. 1780)
John Scott, 1st Earl of Eldon, British barrister and politician, born in Newcastle upon Tyne, Northumberland (d. 1838)
Franz Xaver, Baron Von Zach, Austrian astronomer, born in Pest, Hungary (d. 1832)
James Hewitt, American composer and conductor, born in Dartmoor, England (d. 1827)
Constant Prévost, French geologist, born in Paris (d. 1856)
James Pennethorne, English architect and planner, born in Worcester, Pennethorne (d. 1871)
Gabriel J. Rains, American army officer, Brigadier General (Confederate Army), born in New Bern, North Carolina (d. 1881)
Paul Jones Semmes, American businessman and Brigadier General (Confederate Army), born in Wilkes County, Georgia (d. 1863)
Régis de Trobriand, French aristocrat, lawyer and Bvt Mjr Gen (Union), born in Tours, France (d. 1897)
Apollon Maykov, Russian poet, born in Moscow, Imperial Russia (d. 1897)
Alexander William Campbell, Brigadier General (Confederate Army), born in Nashville, Tennessee (d. 1893)
Garnet Wolseley, 1st Viscount Wolseley, British army general, born in Dublin (d. 1913)
Servaas Daems [Peeter Klein], Flemish writer (Lute & Flute), born in Noorderwijk, Netherlands (d. 1903)
Josef Sittard, German musicologist and music critic, born in Vital, Aachen, Kingdom of Prussia (d. 1903)
John Blair Scribner, American magazine and book publisher, born in New York City (d. 1879)
Inukai Tsuyoshi, Japanese politician (Prime Minister of Japan 1931-32), born in Niwase, Japan (d. 1932)
Edmond Robert Hubert Regout, Dutch industrialist and politician, born in Maastricht, Limburg, Netherlands (d. 1913)
Guido Gasperini, Italian composer, born in Florence, Italy (d. 1942)
Miina Sillanpää, Finnish first female minister and a key figure in the workers' movement, born in Jokioinen, Finland (d. 1952)
Clara Blandick [Clara Blanchard Dickey], American stage and screen character actress (Tom Sawyer; Huckleberry Finn; Wizard of Oz), born on board an American ship in Hong Kong (d. 1962)
Heinrich Otto Wieland, German chemist (bile acids-Nobel 1927), born in Pforzheim, Baden, Germany (d. 1957)
Frank Buchman, American theologist (Moral Re-Armament), born in Pennsburg, Pennsylvania (d. 1961)
Mabel Lucie Attwell, English children's author and illustrator, born in Mile End, London (d. 1964)
Natalia Goncharova, Russian avant-garde artist and painter, born in Nagaevo, Tula Governorate, Russian Empire (d. 1962)
Karl Valentin, Bavarian comedian and film producer, born in Munich (d. 1948)
Tom Longboat, Canadian athlete (Boston Marathon 1907 record time 2:24:24), born in Six Nations Reserve Brantford, Ontario (d. 1949)
Henry Phillips, American businessman (Phillip's screw), born in Bolivar, Mississippi (d. 1958)[1]

Ernö Rapée, Hungarian-American pianist, conductor (Roxt Symphony; Radio City Music Hall, 1932-45), silent and early sound film score composer, and songwriter ("Diane", "Charmaine"), born in Budapest, Hungary (d. 1945)[1]
Armand Călinescu, Prime Minister of Romania, born in Piteşti, Romania (d. 1939)
Daan Boens, Flemish poet (Man who sold his Wife), born in Ostend, Belgium (d. 1977)
Dino Conte Grandi, Italian Fascist politician and delegate to league of nations (1925-32), born in Mordano, Italy (d. 1988)
(James) Stringer Davis, British stage and screen character actor (Murder Most Foul), and veteran of both World Wars, born in Birkenhead, Cheshire, England (d. 1973)
Hassan Fathy, Egyptian architect (New Gourna Village), born in Alexandria, Egypt (d. 1989)
Leo Spies, Russian-born German composer, born in Moscow (d. 1965)
Robert Agnew, American silent movie director, born in Dayton, Kentucky (d. 1983)
Richard Allen, Indian field hockey goalkeeper (Olympic gold 1928), born in Nagpur, India (d. 1969)
Richard Whorf, American actor (Yankee Doodle Dandy; Keeper of Flame), and director (It Happened In Brooklyn), born in Winthrop, Massachusetts (d. 1966)
(Catherine) Rosalind Russell, American stage and screen actress (His Girl Friday; Auntie Mame; Gypsy), born in Waterbury, Connecticut (d. 1976)
Jacques Roumain, Haitian writer (Gouverneurs de la Rosée), born in Port-au-Prince, Haiti (d. 1944)
Marjan Kozina, Slovene composer, born in Novo mesto, Slovenia (d. 1966)
Patience Strong [Winifred Emma May], English poet and journalist, born in Catford, London (d. 1990)
Angela Maria "Geli" Raubal, Hitler's reputed lover and half-niece, born in Linz, Austria-Hungary (d. 1931)
Tony Giffard, 3rd Earl of Halsbury, British crossbencher peer and scientist, born in London (d. 2000)
Walther Vanbeselaere, Flemish art historian, born in Zevekote, Belgium (d. 1988)
Paul Nordoff, American composer (Every Soul Is a Circus), and music therapist (Nordoff-Robbins method), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1977)
William M. Batten, American businessman and CEO (NY Stock Exchange 1976-84), born in Parkersburg, West Virginia (d. 1999)
Christopher Cockerell, English inventor (Hovercraft), born in Cambridge, England (d. 1999)
Jacques Berque, French Islamic scholar, born in Frenda, Algeria (d. 1995)
Austin Andrew Wright, British sculptor, born in Chester, England (d. 1997)
Robert Jacobsen, Danish sculptor (great iron sculptures), born in Copenhagen, Denmark (d. 1993)
Bruno Bettinelli, Italian composer and teacher (Musica per archi), born in Milan, Italy (d. 2004)
Willy-August Linnemann, Danish writer and journalist, born in Harrislee, Germany (d. 1985)
Modibo Keïta, 1st President of Mali (1960-68), born in Bamako Coura, Upper Senegal and Niger (d. 1977)
Walter Hadlee, New Zealand cricket batsman (11 Tests, 1 x 100, 2 x 50; Canterbury; father of Richard, Dayle & Barry), born in Lincoln, Canterbury (d. 2006)
Cecil Blacker, British Army officer, born in York, England (d. 2002)
Robert F. Furchgott, American pharmacologist (co-winner Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine, 1998), and educator, born in Charleston, South Carolina (d. 2009)
Allen Greenwood, British aeronautical engineer and aerospace executive, born in 38 Court Yard, Eltham, London (d. 2009)
Charles Collingwood, American news commentator (CBS, Chronicles), born in Three Rivers, Michigan (d. 1985)
Howard Metzenbaum, American businessman and politician (U.S. Senator from Ohio), born in Cleveland, Ohio (d. 2008)
Robert Merrill [Moishe Miller], American operatic baritone (NY Metropolitan Opera, 1945-76), and actor, born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 2004)
Dorothy Howell Rodham, Mother of US First Lady, Senator and Secretary of State Hillary Rodham Clinton, born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 2011)
Britt Woodman, American jazz trombonist (Duke Ellington; Charles Mingus; Lionel Hampton), born in Los Angeles, California (d. 2000)
Russell E. Train, American environmentalist and administrator (EPA, WWF), born in Jamestown, Rhode Island (d. 2012)
Bobby Wanzer, American basketball guard (NBA All-Star 1952–56; Rochester Royals) and coach (NBA All-Star Game 1957), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 2016)
Nina Vyroubova, Russian-French ballerina (Pavlova Prize, 1957), born in Gurzuf, Crimea, Ukrainian S.S.R. (d. 2007)
Irwin Bazelon, American composer (Duo for Viola), born in Evanston, Illinois (d. 1995)
Samuel Lee Gravely Jr., American naval officer, First African American fleet commander, born in Richmond, Virginia (d. 2004)
Elizabeth Jolley, British-born Australian writer (Palomino), born in Birmingham, England (d. 2007)
John Lea, British vice-admiral, born in Shimla, India (d. 2015)
Dennis Weaver, American actor (Chester-Gunsmoke, Duel, Battered), born in Joplin, Missouri (d. 2006)
Antonio Puchades, Spanish soccer midfielder (23 caps; Valencia 256 games), born in Sueca, Spain (d. 2013)
Chuck Thompson, American jazz be-bop drummer (Erroll Garner; Hampton Hawes), born in New York City (d. 1982)
Nan Leslie [Nan Coppage], American actress (Kings Row, The Californians), born in Los Angeles, California (d. 2000)
Robert Earl Hughes, American man who became heaviest known human (485 kg), born in Monticello, Missouri (d. 1958)
Geoffrey Palmer, British actor (A Fish Called Wanda, The Madness of King George, Butterflies, Smacks & Thistle), born in London, England (d. 2020)
Priscilla Morrill, American actress (Edie Grant-Mary Tyler Moore), born in Medford, Massachusetts (d. 1994)
Lloyd Lambert, American bassist (Guitar Slim), born in Thilbodaux, Louisiana (d. 1995)
Paul Greening, Royal Navy officer and master of Queen's household, born in Steyning, Sussex, England (d. 2008)
Ruth Westheimer (née Karola Ruth Siegel) German-American sex therapist and media personality (Dr Ruth), born in Wiesenfeld, Germany (d. 2024)[1]
Teddy Kotick, American jazz double bassist (Phil Woods; Charlie Parker; Bill Evans), born in Haverhill, Massachusetts (d. 1986)
Whakahuihui Vercoe, New Zealand Anglican clergyman (Archbishop of New Zealand, 2004-06; Bishop of Aotearoa, 1981-2004), and  Māori rights advocate, born in Tōrere, North Island, New Zealand (d. 2007)
Andor Kovács, Hungarian classical guitarist, and composer, born in Budapest, Hungary (d. 1989)
Karolos Papoulias, President of Greece (2005-15), born in Ioannina, Greece (d. 2021)
Pierre Danos, French rugby union halfback (17 Tests; RC Toulon, AS Béziers), born in Toulouse, France (d. 2023)
Bill Treacher, British actor (EastEnders, 1985-96 - "Arthur Fowler"), born in London, England (d. 2022)
Morgana King [Messina], American jazz singer ("Dindi"; "It's A Quiet Thing"), and actress (The Godfather; The Godfather Part II), born in Pleasantville, New York (d. 2018)
Pentti Raitio, Finnish composer (‘Lemminkainen Gatecrashes at Pohjola’), born in Pieksämäki, Finland (d. 2014)
César Bolaños, Peruvian electro-acoustic and experimental musician and composer, born in Lima, Peru (d. 2012)
John Drew Barrymore, American actor (Pantomine Quiz), born in Beverly Hills, California (d. 2004)
John McNamara, American MLB manager (American League Manager of the Year 1986; Boston Red Sox), born in Sacramento, California (d. 2020)
Maurice Shadbolt, New Zealand writer (Season of the Jew), born in Auckland, New Zealand (d. 2004)

Oliver Nelson, American saxophonist, jazz and soundtrack composer, arranger, bandleader, and record producer (The Blues and the Abstract Truth; The Six Million Dollar Man), born in St. Louis, Missouri (d. 1975)
Dmitry Mindiashvili, Soviet and Russian wrestling coach (coach Olympic champions Ivan Yariguin 1972, 76 and Buvaisar Satiev (1992, 96, 2004), born in Tbilisi, Georgia (d. 2021)
John Sparrow, British banker and bureaucrat (Chairman of British Horserace Betting Levy Board, 1991-99), born in London (d. 2020)
Carl Fredrik Reutersward, Swedish artist (knotted-gun sculpture), born in Stockholm (d. 2016)
